original_reasoning: Arabic صخم 'standing upright, erect posture' vs σχῆμα 'form/posture/figure'; conceptual overlap on bodily posture/form

الأنس: الإنسي=الأيسر (the left/easier side), ما أقبل عليك (what faces you). Also root أنس=familiarity, ease, comfort. γαληνός=calm, serene (especially of the sea). Both orbit the concept of tranquility, ease, and gentleness. The emotional quality of peaceful comfort connects them.
Arabic includes becoming irritable from hunger or teeth being worn/soured, and Greek means abusive or insulting people; irritability can plausibly drift into verbal abuse.
If the Greek term belongs to the λώβη field of harm and ruin, Arabic الداهية shares a strong negative disaster core, giving a plausible semantic chain.
The sense of forcefully overwhelming or fully entering preserves a plausible domination chain toward being tamed or subdued.
Arabic hostility or dispute can yield a split relation; Greek means apart or asunder.
خنط is distress or pressing, while εἴχοντο is they held or were held; pressure and being held form a plausible semantic chain.

اشرحف=تهيأ لمحاربته (prepared himself for combat, moved toward confrontation). προσέρχομαι=to come/go toward, approach. Both involve directed movement toward someone. The Arabic specifies hostile approach, Greek is neutral, but the 'approaching' core is solid.
